Search results: --- SOURCE 1: Unlocking the Power of Virtue in Leadership --- URL: https://capacity-building.com/unlocking-the-power-of-virtue-in-leadership/ SUMMARY: By embracing virtues like honesty, integrity, humility, and compassion, leaders can inspire trust, foster a positive culture, and lead their -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --- SOURCE 2: Introduction to Cultivating Virtue and Leadership --- URL: https://jefflockert.com/introduction/ SUMMARY: I believe that successful leaders develop both the technical and character-based aspects of their leadership but all leaders make a foundational choice. FULL CONTENT: # Introduction to Cultivating Virtue and Leadership I have spent more than 25 years in the personal and professional study of leadership. [![Jeff Speaking](http://jefflockert.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/Jeff-Speaking-300x300.jpg)](http://jefflockert.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/Jeff-Speaking.jpg)And believe me there is a lot written about it. When I wrote this introduction, there were over 479 million hits on Google using the keyword leadership.  On Amazon.com there were currently 121,231 books you could order related to the topic. In many ways it is wonderful to have so much information available, but in other ways it’s overwhelming. ![Jeff Speaking](http://jefflockert.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/Jeff-Speaking-300x300.jpg) I am also a father of six children and I have many hopes for them: to pick up their clothes, share their toys, and eat their dinner, and over the long term, to become mature adults, find a meaningful career, marry well, love others and be loved themselves, etc. But one of my greatest hopes is to cultivate virtuous leadership within them. What do I mean by this? In my study and application of leadership I find that there are two approaches; one is primarily technique based and the other primarily character based. The technical approach involves skills, tools, frameworks, and templates in a whole host of important leadership settings. These might include things like giving a speech, making a plan, running a meeting, and working in a team. These are very important elements of effective leadership. In many ways these might be summed up as “what a leader does.” The character-based approach involves something very personal, something we might describe as “who a leader is.” It’s harder to describe using finite activities because it transcends specific activities and extends to a leader’s make up, their character, their virtues or lack of virtue, their thinking, and their worldview. I believe that successful leaders develop both the technical and character-based aspects of their leadership but all leaders make a foundational choice. People either choose to put character as the foundation of their leadership or they choose to put technique as the foundation. This decision, whether conscious or unconscious, is the single most important factor in determining the long term success of a leader. It will orient their study of leadership, their consistency and effectiveness, their response in the face of adversity and difficulties, and ultimately, their influence in people’s lives. As a father my hope is to cultivate character and virtue as the foundation for my children’s leadership development. Various articles and posts will explain why I have this hope and introduce further reflections on leadership. [Click for Full List of Article Titles](http://jefflockert.com/blog/title-index/)

See this blog post I wrote on how to do that. · Do the newspaper -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --- SOURCE 4: Four Virtues That Our Leaders Need --- URL: https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/four_virtues_that_our_leaders_need SUMMARY: Research suggests that cultivating character promotes a flourishing life—and leaders with character help build a flourishing society. Our mission was also a research question: How can we educate a generation of wise thinkers and good leaders who will further the flourishing of individuals, organizations, and societies around the world? The idea that underlies the Oxford Character Project, that virtuous leadership is a key driver of human flourishing and the common good, is far from original. As we head into our second decade, we are building partnerships for the future, planning new research on character-based leadership in politics, business, and sport, and developing programs that will support a new generation of leaders at Oxford and around the world. [Give Now](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/donate) SKIP TO: [Header](#logo) | [Log in](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/_ajax/login-popup) | [Register](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/_ajax/register-popup) | [Navigation](#skip-nav) | [Main Content](#skip-main) | [Footer](#skip-footer) **Greater Good** [Science Center](https://ggsc.berkeley.edu "Greater Good Science Center") • [Magazine](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu "Greater Good Magazine") • [In Action](https://ggia.berkeley.edu "Greater Good In Action") • [In Education](https://ggie.berkeley.edu "Greater Good In Education") [Sign up for our newsletters](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/_ajax/newsletter-popup) [Greater Good Magazine](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/) [nav menu](#) [Education](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/education) Articles & More [Scroll To Top](#) # Four Virtues That Our Leaders Need Research suggests that cultivating character promotes a flourishing life—and leaders with character help build a flourishing society. By [Edward Brooks](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/edward_brooks) | July 28, 2025 [Facebook](/#facebook) [Bluesky](/#bluesky) [LinkedIn](/#linkedin) [Email](/#email) [Share](https://www.addtoany.com/share#url=https%3A%2F%2Fgreatergood.berkeley.edu%2Farticle%2Fitem%2Ffour_virtues_that_our_leaders_need&title=Four%20Virtues%20That%20Our%20Leaders%20Need) [Facebook](/#facebook) [Bluesky](/#bluesky) [LinkedIn](/#linkedin) [Email](/#email) [Share](https://www.addtoany.com/share#url=https%3A%2F%2Fgreatergood.berkeley.edu%2Farticle%2Fitem%2Ffour_virtues_that_our_leaders_need&title=Four%20Virtues%20That%20Our%20Leaders%20Need) * [*Print*](#) * [*Bookmark*](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/_ajax/login-popup) It started, as many things in Oxford do, with a conversation after lunch. It was the spring of 2013, and a group of professors gathered in a college common room. Their discussion turned to a troubling theme—a widely reported crisis of leadership across sectors and around the world. In case after case, in banking, politics, business, journalism, technology, sport, the military, and beyond, leaders were failing to uphold the standards of integrity and responsibility demanded by their role. The heart of the problem was not a lack of leadership skills or technical expertise; it came down to culture and character. For institutions like the University of Oxford, the responsibility was impossible to ignore. After all, we take pride in educating students who go on to hold positions of influence around the world. As one faculty member put it, “there is no doubt that Oxford produces thinkers and leaders, but are we producing *wise* thinkers and *good* leaders?” This conversation and the generous support of the Templeton World Charity Foundation gave rise to the Oxford Character Project. Our mission was also a research question: How can we educate a generation of wise thinkers and good leaders who will further the flourishing of individuals, organizations, and societies around the world? ## How character promotes flourishing Advertisement X The idea that underlies the Oxford Character Project, that virtuous leadership is a key driver of human flourishing and the common good, is far from original. It was a premise of classical thinkers such as Aristotle and Confucius, a driving force of the European Renaissance, and an organizing principle of higher education through to the beginning of the 20th century. However, such an emphasis on character did not fare well through the last century. Modernization was based on technological progress and the design of systems and structures to optimize economic outcomes. Values and virtues—once foundational to education and public life—were increasingly limited to the private realm. In the modern world, public life was to be ruled by facts, not by values. Fast forward to the present, and a strong distinction between measurable facts and intangible values is no longer as plausible, not least because of huge strides in the science of human flourishing. Just this year, a paper in *Nature* announced the [first results of the Global Flourishing Study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41562-025-02213-6), a longitudinal study of over 200,000 people in more than 20 countries to explore aspects and drivers of flourishing. Led by researchers at Baylor University and Harvard’s Human Flourishing Program, the study defines flourishing as “the relative attainment of a state in which all aspects of a person’s life are good, including the contexts in which that person lives.” Alongside other dimensions of flourishing—mental and physical health, financial and material stability, meaning, and close social relationships—are character and virtue. Virtues (positive character qualities) such as gratitude, justice, wisdom, courage, temperance, kindness, and hope are at the heart of flourishing lives and societies. Dorota Weziak-Bialowolska and her colleagues conducted studies in the U.S. and Mexico to examine the [impact of character on flourishing](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0890117120964083). They found that people with good character (orientation to promote good) tend to be more satisfied with life and happier, report better mental and physical health, and feel more socially connected and purposeful. Character is a component of human flourishing. But flourishing is not simply an individual phenomenon. It is supported by all kinds of institutions: families, schools, health care, transportation, hospitality, media, culture, business, construction, community organizations, sports clubs, local and national governments, technology providers, banks, and religious communities. This is where leadership comes in. All these institutions, and more, are central to the flourishing of individuals and societies. But they need ethical and effective leadership—character-based leadership—to contribute to the greater good.   In 2023, [we investigated character-based leadership around the world](https://oxfordcharacter.org/research/character-global-leadership-report-2023), identifying 720 papers that report on the impact of character-based leadership. They find benefits for individuals (e.g., well-being, sense of meaning, job satisfaction, creativity and innovation), organizations (e.g., ethical climate, organizational performance, team cohesion, interpersonal trust), and society (e.g., corruption prevention, social justice, sustainability). ## Purpose, courage, love, and hope Since 2014, we have been conducting research, developing programs, and building partnerships to advance character-based leadership. Just this year, we released [Leading with Character](http://www.leadingwithcharacter.com/), a free online course developed together with the Legatum Foundation and the Human Flourishing Program at Harvard. The course was designed by a global group of academics and practitioners to support people around the world to be better leaders and build a better world. It has so far been taken up by 2,000 learners in 122 countries. The course focuses on four central virtues: purpose, courage, love, and hope. If leaders were to cultivate these virtues, if we could even strengthen them by a few percent, we believe there would be a marked impact on leadership performance and societal flourishing.     [**Purpose** is defined by researcher Bill Damon and his colleagues](https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1207/S1532480XADS0703_2) as “a forward-looking intention to pursue goals meaningful to the self and beneficial to others.” Purpose supports vision and direction, enabling leaders to inspire followers, unite strategy with mission, and pursue goals that advance the common good. There is a close relationship between values and purpose. We have found that helping students to reflect on their [values](https://ggia.berkeley.edu/practice/life_crafting), [strengths](https://ggia.berkeley.edu/practice/use_your_strengths), and goals has enabled them to clarify their sense of purpose. **Courage** in leadership involves acting in accordance with one’s values in the face of difficulty, risk, or discomfort. Leadership is tough, and fear of failure or loss can cause leaders to shrink back from the actions and decisions needed to build a better world. Courage enables leaders to step forward. In the Leading with Character course, we focus on the need for courage in everyday leadership contexts. As one student put it, “I’ve realized that courage doesn’t have to be something that is loud or bold; anything you do in life that takes effort towards goodness is courage.” * ### Greater Good Educators Program Be an educator for the greater good. Join our virtual Community of Practice program for educators. [Register](https://ggie.berkeley.edu/course/greater-good-educators-program-2025-2026/) **Love** is a word rarely used in relation to leadership. It should be. Understood as a commitment to the good of others, leading with love fosters security, trust, and collaboration. It is expressed as leaders put others’ interests before their own, looking for the best in others and acting with kindness even when making hard decisions. As one student put it, “I have learned that love in leadership is not about sentimentality but about creating meaningful connections, valuing people, and fostering an environment where others feel seen, heard, and empowered.” **Hope** is not simply a positive expectation; that’s optimism. Hope is like a muscle that is developed by repeated practice. It is a [focused attention on the possibility of a good future](https://whatworkswellbeing.org/blog/hope-and-optimism-distinctions-and-deepening-conceptions/), especially in the face of difficulty, challenges, and uncertainties. Hope empowers leaders to continually work toward that future. As Napoleon is famously said to have put it, leaders are also “dealers in hope”; they can [cultivate hope in communities and organizations](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/00018392211055506) by the stories they tell and examples they set. ## Good leadership for a better world Leaders certainly need expertise in strategy, technology, risk management, and communication. Virtuous leadership—leading with purpose, courage, love, and hope—elevates these competencies as a force for good. Our work at the Oxford Character Project is built on the premise that the leaders we need to face the many challenges and uncertainties of our time require the highest levels of excellence in both competence and character. As we head into our second decade, we are building partnerships for the future, planning new research on character-based leadership in politics, business, and sport, and developing programs that will support a new generation of leaders at Oxford and around the world. ## About the Author * ### [Edward Brooks](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/edward_brooks) **Edward Brooks, D.Phil.**, is executive director of the [Oxford Character Project](http://www.oxfordcharacter.org/), director of the Programme for Global Leadership, and titular associate professor of leadership studies in the Department of Politics and International Relations at the University of Oxford. ## You May Also Enjoy * ### [Three Things That Influence Our Character](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/three_things_that_influence_our_character) By [Cristy Guleserian](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/cristy_guleserian) | November 22, 2023 * ### [How Communities of Practice Can Support Teachers](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/how_communities_of_practice_can_support_teachers) By [Danny Mucinskas](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/danny_mucinskas) | May 12, 2025 * ### [How Educators Can Help Make a Kinder World](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/how_educators_can_help_make_a_kinder_world) By [Vicki Zakrzewski](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/vicki_zakrzewski) | February 2, 2021 * ### [Is Character the Key to Success?](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/is_character_the_key_to_success) By [Vicki Zakrzewski](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/vicki_zakrzewski) | September 26, 2012 * ### [Four Character Strengths That Can Help Kids Learn](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/four_character_strengths_that_can_help_kids_learn) By [Carol Lloyd](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/carol_lloyd) | November 15, 2021 * ### [How West Point Is Expanding Character Education](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/how_west_point_is_expanding_character_education) By [Elise Dykhuis](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/elise_dykhuis), [Ryan Erbe](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/ryan_erbe), [Jeffrey D. Peterson](https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/jeffrey_peterson) | July 18, 2023 ## Comments -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --- SOURCE 5: Cultivating Core Leadership Virtues | by Not Theoretical --- URL: https://medium.com/@kcbarr/cultivating-core-leadership-virtues-87444302daaf SUMMARY: Develop Personalized Growth Plans: You can work with each individual to identify personal strengths and areas for growth, and create FULL CONTENT: Sign up [Sign in](/m/signin?operation=login&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40kcbarr%2Fcultivating-core-leadership-virtues-87444302daaf&source=post_page---top_nav_layout_nav-----------------------global_nav------------------) Sign up [Sign in](/m/signin?operation=login&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40kcbarr%2Fcultivating-core-leadership-virtues-87444302daaf&source=post_page---top_nav_layout_nav-----------------------global_nav------------------) ![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*dmbNkD5D-u45r44go_cf0g.png) # Cultivating Core Leadership Virtues ## Why settle for managing when you can lead like a coach? Coach Leadership is about inspiring, mentoring, and co-creating with your team to achieve extraordinary results. ![Not Theoretical](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*kW_eV7suNwFMiaK5hBQ3Yw.png) -- Listen Share In today’s article, discover how adopting this leadership style can drive personal growth and organizational success. Are you ready to make the shift? 💼 ![]() # IN TODAY’S ARTICLE: ## 1. 📖“10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times” Overview ## 2. 💪Put Three Concepts into Practice Today ## 3. 👀 See the Concepts in Action! ## 4.🌳How to Grow Your Professional Network in 30 Seconds ## 5. 🧠Interested in Expanding Your Mind More? Check out these. *i Some links on this page are affiliate links* # 1. 📖 “10 LEADERSHIP VIRTUES FOR DISRUPTIVE TIMES” OVERVIEW “We will always have a choice: Either fold up and go home or rise up and create a better world.” ![]() If you’re grappling with leadership in today’s ever-changing landscape, you might want to check out Tom Ziglar’s “10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge.” This book offers a fresh take on leading like a coach, laying out 10 practical virtues from kindness to perseverance. With insights, challenges, and unique techniques like the STICKY method of problem-solving, Ziglar presents a guide that’s both accessible and relevant. As someone who’s looking to lead effectively, *don’t you wonder how these virtues could transform your team’s performance in a world where disruption is the norm?* ***Audiobook (6 hours, 49 minutes) and book available on Amazon:*** ## 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge ### 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge - Kindle edition by… amzn.to [***BLINKIST (here)****:*](https://blinkist-us.pxf.io/e4L3MO) *17 minute in-depth audio book summary* # 2. 💪PUT THREE (3) KEY CONCEPTS\* FROM THE BOOK INTO PRACTICE TODAY Let’s dive into each concept and learn practical ways to put them into practice: **1️⃣Aligning Leadership Practices** **2️⃣Coach Leadership** **3️⃣Cultivating Virtues** *\*You’ll find several more concepts in the book* # 1️⃣ALIGNING LEADERSHIP PRACTICES “When you are able to stand firm like a lighthouse in the middle of a storm, you create an incredible confidence in your team that leads to productivity and performance.” ![]() This concept focuses on bringing together leadership strategies across different levels of an organization. It emphasizes the importance of shared values and principles to build trust and foster collaboration, particularly during disruptive times. You learn from this concept that alignment in leadership practices can create a unified, resilient organization capable of thriving in uncertainty. *Page-to-practice ideas for you:* *Page-to-practice ideas tailored for:* ## 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge ### 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge - Kindle edition by… amzn.to # 2️⃣COACH LEADERSHIP “One of the first things a leader should do is illuminate the purpose, mission and why of their organization.” ![]() The concept of Coach Leadership encourages a shift from traditional management roles to a more engaging and interactive approach. Instead of directing and controlling, you adopt the role of a coach, inspiring your team and working alongside them. This approach fosters creativity and ownership among team members and allows for more agile responses to change. You learn from this concept how to utilize coaching techniques to motivate and co-create with your team. *Page-to-practice ideas for you:* *Page-to-practice ideas tailored for:* ## 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge ### 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge - Kindle edition by… amzn.to # 3️⃣CULTIVATING VIRTUES “Humility is not thinking less of yourself; it is thinking of yourself less”.- C.S. Lewis ![]() Cultivating virtues refers to the intentional development of positive qualities such as kindness, respect, strength, and vulnerability within leaders and team members. These virtues are essential in building positive relationships, enhancing collaboration, and driving innovation. You learn from this concept how to integrate these virtues into various aspects of your professional life, whether it’s personal growth, employee engagement, or overall organizational culture. By focusing on these virtues, you can adapt and thrive in disruptive times. *Page-to-practice ideas for you:* *Page-to-practice ideas tailored for:* 🤔How have you seen the cultivation of virtues like kindness, respect, and empathy make a difference in your workplace or community? ## 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge ### 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge - Kindle edition by… amzn.to # 3. 👀SEE THE CONCEPTS IN ACTION🏰📜 # 🏰Read the Short Story ***‘Alexander’s Odyssey: A Voyage of Visionary Leadership’*** In the heart of a bustling metropolis, there stood a dynamic corporation, renowned for its cutting-edge solutions and vibrant workplace culture. At its core was Alexander, a forward-thinking business owner whose leadership style was marked by sagacity, compassion, and innovation. This narrative unfolds around how Alexander embraced the principles of **Aligning Leadership Practices, Coach Leadership, and Cultivating Virtues**, steering his organization towards a legacy of success and positive impact. Alexander believed that to steer his company through the competitive tides of the industry, he needed to embody the qualities of a lighthouse — unwavering, guiding, and enlightening. He initiated this journey by **aligning leadership practices** throughout his organization. Crafting a shared vision that echoed in the hearts of every employee, he intertwined the company’s objectives with their personal ambitions. He nurtured trust through transparency, fostering a workspace where open dialogue was not just welcomed but essential. Alexander’s consistent application of leadership strategies across all teams ensured that every division, like interconnected gears, worked in perfect synergy towards shared objectives. Embracing the mantle of **coach leadership**, Alexander evolved from a traditional managerial role to one of inspiration and joint effort. He took on the role of a mentor, investing in his employees’ holistic development. He encouraged a collaborative environment, working shoulder to shoulder with his team, valuing their contributions, and co-creating innovative solutions. His establishment of a culture rich in feedback meant that growth was a continuous journey, and every voice was significant. Perhaps the most transformative aspect of Alexander’s leadership was his commitment to **cultivating virtues.** He highlighted core values such as respect, empathy, and integrity, weaving them into the fabric of the company’s ethos. Personalized development plans for each team member ensured that individual talents were acknowledged and fostered. By championing a culture of empathy and respect, Alexander crafted a workplace where every individual felt acknowledged and integral, thereby enhancing teamwork and sparking innovation. Under Alexander’s stewardship, the company not only flourished in times of disruption but also became an exemplar of resilience and unity. His story is a testament to the transformative power of visionary leadership and the profound influence of integrating fundamental leadership principles into everyday practice. ***Audiobook (6 hours, 49 minutes) and book available on Amazon:*** ## 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge ### 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge — Kindle edition by… amzn.to [***BLINKIST (here)****:*](https://blinkist-us.pxf.io/e4L3MO) *17 minute in-depth audio book summary* # 📜 Read the Fable ***The Lighthouse and the Tempest*** In a bustling coastal village nestled between the wild sea and the towering cliffs, there stood a lighthouse known as Beacon Point. This lighthouse was not just a guiding light for ships; it was a symbol of hope, strength, and leadership for the villagers who lived under its watchful glow. ![]() The lighthouse was tended by an old keeper named Fran, who was known for his unwavering dedication to his duty. Fran had three young apprentices: Andy, Alexis, and Joshua. Each of them had different strengths — Andy was wise and patient, Alexis was quick and resourceful, and Joshua was kind-hearted and brave. One fateful day, a fierce tempest began to brew on the horizon. The waves grew monstrous, and the winds howled with terrifying force. The villagers, fearing for their lives and the safety of their ships, looked to the lighthouse for guidance. **Aligning Leadership Practices:** Fran knew that the strength of the lighthouse would depend on the unity of their efforts. He gathered Andy, Alexis, and Joshua and spoke to them with calm authority. “In times like these, we must work together, aligning our efforts like the gears in a well-oiled machine. Alexis, you will ensure the light burns brightly and continuously. Andy, you will reinforce the lighthouse structure, and Joshua, you will gather the villagers and keep them safe.” The apprentices, understanding the importance of their individual roles, set out to perform their tasks with precision and determination. **Coach Leadership:** Fran did not merely give orders; he worked alongside his apprentices, guiding them with wisdom and encouragement. He taught Alexis how to adjust the light to cut through the thick fog, showing her the subtleties of the craft. He praised Andy for his careful reinforcement of the lighthouse, offering gentle suggestions to improve his work. With Joshua, Fran stood in the storm, helping to gather and reassure the frightened villagers, sharing his own experiences of weathering past tempests. Fran’s presence and mentorship turned fear into courage, and chaos into coordinated action. **Cultivating Virtues:** As the storm raged on, Fran, Andy, Alexis, and Joshua displayed virtues that were essential to their survival. Alexis, in the face of the relentless winds, remained patient and resourceful, adapting her techniques to ensure the light shone without falter. Andy, though battered by the elements, showed perseverance, reinforcing the lighthouse’s walls with steady hands and a strong heart. Joshua, amidst the cries and fears of the villagers, demonstrated empathy and kindness, comforting the young and old alike, reminding them that they were not alone. Through their combined efforts, the lighthouse stood firm, and its light shone bright, guiding the ships safely to shore. The storm eventually passed, leaving the village intact and the villagers deeply grateful. The next day, as the sun rose over the calm sea, Fran gathered his apprentices and the villagers together. “Remember,” he said, “it is not just the strength of the stone and light that protects us, but the strength of our unity, our guidance, and our virtues. These are the true beacons in times of darkness.” ## Moral: The moral of the fable is that in times of great challenge, the alignment of leadership, the nurturing of others through coaching, and the cultivation of virtues such as patience, perseverance, and kindness are what truly guide us through the storm. These elements, when combined, create a resilient and thriving community. ***Audiobook (6 hours, 49 minutes) and book available on Amazon:*** ## 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge ### 10 Leadership Virtues for Disruptive Times: Coaching Your Team Through Immense Change and Challenge — Kindle edition by… amzn.to [***BLINKIST (here)****:*](https://blinkist-us.pxf.io/e4L3MO) *17 minute in-depth audio book summary* # 4. 🌳GROW YOUR PROFESSIONAL NETWORK IN 30 SECONDS Feel free to send me a connection request on LinkedIn — it’s always great to meet new professionals! # 5. 🧠INTERESTED IN EXPANDING YOUR MIND MORE? IMAGE 3: URL: https://i.pinimg.com/originals/54/06/68/5406680bda2123b0d38d90e07850d824.jpg DESCRIPTION: The illustration presents various virtues essential for life, including patience, loyalty, responsibility, hard work, kindness, respect, humility, and honesty, each accompanied by a brief description of its significance and benefits. IMAGE 4: URL: https://i.pinimg.com/originals/21/a8/44/21a844d94420e41c5d6162e4d52369b8.png DESCRIPTION: A table presents various moral virtues categorized as vices of deficiency, virtues as a mean, and vices of excess, illustrating the balance between extremes in moral character. IMAGE 5: URL: https://wegrowteachers.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/9-Intellectual-Virtues-.png DESCRIPTION: A list of nine intellectual virtues, including curiosity, humility, and courage, is arranged vertically beside a dark background with a light-colored section, featuring an icon of a hand holding a lightbulb and the title "9 Intellectual Virtues To Equip Students for Success in the Modern World." --------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Leaders should embody virtues such as honesty, integrity, and compassion, as these traits establish trust and foster a positive organizational culture, enhancing overall effectiveness.
Transparent leadership involves open communication about decisions and processes, which builds trust, encourages accountability, and aligns team efforts with shared organizational goals.
Facilitating conversations about ethics encourages team members to engage in moral reasoning, helping them navigate challenges while reinforcing a culture of integrity and responsibility.
